category|Category:Oominakami-jinja (Mitoyo, Kagawa)}} [[JA:大水上神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11436783}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Oominakami Shrine |Image = [[Image:Oominakami Jinjya-Mitoyo01.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454}}{{ill|lt=Mitoyo City|Q873578|WD=Q873578}} 2677-2 Hagata, Takase-cho |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deities = Oominakami Daimyojin, Homutawake no Mikoto, Munakata Okami |Founded = Unknown |Shrine Rank = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (Engishiki-listed small shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287}}, {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya#Ninomiya, Sannomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya}}, former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Annual Festival = |Rituals = |Main Hall = |Other Name = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Oominakami Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oominakami Shrine''' (おおみなかみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454}}{{ill|lt=Mitoyo City|Q873578|WD=Q873578}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the Engishiki, and under the former {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank}} it was classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is regarded as the Ninomiya of {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682}}. === Overview === The goshuin (shrine stamp) reception desk is open on Saturdays and Sundays, or Sundays only, from around 1:30 PM to 4:00 PM; however, this may change depending on the month, so please check the Facebook page below in advance. During events such as the Great Purification Ceremony and kagura performances in early July, and outdoor taiko drum group live performances in October, the shrine grounds are illuminated with traditional Japanese umbrellas and bamboo lights. === History === The shrine began with the worship of two iwakura (sacred rock formations), one beside Unagi Fuchi behind the main hall and another behind Sen’go Ōji Shrine. Its founding is said to date back either to the Yayoi period or the Kofun period, and it is recorded in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō as the Ninomiya of Sanuki Province. Since ancient times, rainmaking rituals have been conducted here during times of drought. In the medieval period, the local lord Kondō Masamitsu invited Hachiman deity to the shrine. Together with Oominakami Daimyojin, Hachiman Ōkami and Mishima Ryūjin were enshrined, and it came to be known as “Ninomiya Sansha.” It became a shrine practicing syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, with Kiyozumi-dera and Jingū-ji serving as bettō temples, and prospered under the protection of the Kondō family. It is also said that Kūkai (Kōbō Daishi) visited the shrine before departing for Tang China, performed rituals, and composed dialogic waka poems with the three deities. Kūkai: “Having come from afar to worship, may you grant me the power of the three deities and protect me. In the early summer rains, with thoughts of the mountains, under the traveling sky I long for rest and come before you.” Reply from Hachiman Ōkami: “I am even now the god before the bow; know that I shall protect you even unto China.” Upper verse by Oominakami Daimyojin: “May your coming and going be at peace upon the sea of the sky,” Lower verse by Mishima Ryūjin: “I, the pure waters above, am the Dragon Deity.” From “Origins of Ninomiya Sansha” (compiled in the mid-Edo period) In 1184 (Genryaku 1), during the Genpei War, both the Minamoto and Taira clans dedicated written prayers to the shrine. In 1427 (Ōei 34), when the shrine was damaged, the emperor bore the cost of its reconstruction. It is said to have been burned by the Chōsokabe forces during the Sengoku period. The present buildings were rebuilt during the {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341}} era and renovated after the Meiji period. === Enshrined Deities === * Oominakami Daimyojin (sometimes identified with {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamazumi no Mikoto}}, though there are various theories) * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake no Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Munakata Three Goddesses|Q10948069|WD=Q10948069|lt=Munakata Ōkami}} === Shrine Grounds === [[File:Oominakami shrine 20240713 0.jpg|thumb|Main shrine worship hall]] * First Torii Gate: Located beside the prefectural road. * Suisen Bridge * Second Torii Gate: Beyond this lies a dense sacred area. * Misogi purification site * Shimenawa Stone: The sacred rope at this shrine is also straight across the worship hall. * Zuishin Gate * Third Torii Gate * Shigure Lantern: Prefecturally designated cultural property * Main Shrine Worship Hall and Main Hall * Ema Hall, Archery Range, Shrine Office * Unagi Fuchi (Ryūō Fuchi): Remains of rainmaking rituals. It is said that black and white {{ill|lt=eels|Q1422437|WD=Q1422437}} live here. If a black eel appears during rainmaking, rain will fall; if a white eel appears, there will be drought; and if a {{ill|lt=crab|Q40802|WD=Q40802}} appears, strong winds will come.From explanatory signboards installed throughout the shrine grounds, and below as well. * Iwakura (Meoto Iwa): Ancient sacred objects central to Oominakami worship. A stone shrine stands atop the lower rock of a two-tiered formation. * Oominakami Mountain Stream Valley: A dark, densely wooded valley along the Miyagawa River, a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Saita River|Q11634632|WD=Q11634632}}. * Haiku and Tanka Monuments: {{ill|lt=Mizuhara Shūōshi|Q1940999|WD=Q1940999}} “Though not famed as a tea region, none compare to its fresh tea”; {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Seishi|Q2599126|WD=Q2599126}} “Fine is the new tea, enveloped in the mist of Oominakami.” Inscription of Kōbō Daishi’s prayer: “May your coming and going be peaceful upon the sea of the sky; I, the pure waters above, am the Dragon Deity.” From the Ninomiya records. === Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines === * Sen’go Ōji Shrine: Located up stone steps behind the main shrine grounds. A wooden shrine stands atop the lower rock beneath a three-tiered iwakura. Enshrines Izanagi no Mikoto and Izanami no Mikoto. ** Musubi Shrine: Enshrines the deity of binding (Musubi), connecting all prayers. * Shishamiya: Located beside the mikoshi storehouse on the left of the main grounds. Enshrines Taira no Norimori, Taira no Tsunemori, Taira no Sukemori, and Taira no Arimori. * Toyoha Shrine: Located on a hill to the left of the main grounds. Enshrines Aoba Ōkami, Kayano Hime no Mikoto, Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, and Sukunahikona no Mikoto. ** Ushigami Shrine: Enshrines Uke Mochi no Kami. ** Aramitama Shrine: Enshrines Ōmononushi no Kami. * Haraedo Shrine: Located to the right after passing through the Second Torii Gate. Enshrines Seoritsuhime no Kami, Hayakitsuhime no Kami, Ibukido Nushi no Kami, and Hayasasurahime no Kami. === Cultural Properties === ; National Historic Site * Ninomiya Kiln Ruins: Located at the mountain base outside the right side of the shrine grounds. Two kilns—No.1 (climbing kiln) on the right and No.2 (flat kiln) on the left—have yielded roof tiles, cups, and inkstones. A site spanning from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period. Designated April 25, 1932. ; Prefecturally Designated Tangible Cultural Property * Shigure Lantern: Total height 246 cm, made of tuff stone, created in 1346 (Kōei 4) by a craftsman of the highest rank “Hōkyō.” Designated April 9, 1963. ; Prefecturally Designated Natural Monument * Oominakami Shrine Juniper Tree: Located 20 meters north of the First Torii at the Otabisho. Height 12 m, branch spread 14 m, trunk circumference 4.4 m at breast height. Designated March 1976. === Access === From {{ill|lt=National Route 377|Q6157382|WD=Q6157382}}, take {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefectural Road 218 Saita-Kamitakase Line|Q11667499|WD=Q11667499}}. About 15 minutes from Sanuki-Toyonaka IC. Parking is available for 15 cars in front of the Zuishin Gate and 16 cars along the prefectural road in front of the First Torii. Entry to the shrine grounds is prohibited from 7:00 PM to around 5:00 AM due to the risk of wild boar sightings. === Notes === {{Notes help}} {{Reflist}} === External Links === {{Commonscat|Oominakami-jinja_(Mitoyo, Kagawa)}} * {{Instagram|minakaminomori}} * {{Facebook|ominakami}} * [Oominakami Shrine] - Mitoyo City {{Shinto navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:oominakamishinja}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oominakami Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oominakami Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Ni-no-Miya]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sanuki Province]] [[Category:香川県の神社]] [[Category:讃岐国の式内社]] [[Category:香川県の旧県社]] [[Category:三豊市の歴史]] [[Category:讃岐国|社2]] [[Category:三豊市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]]

category|Category:Oominakami-jinja (Mitoyo, Kagawa)}} [[JA:大水上神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11436783}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Oominakami Shrine |Image = [[Image:Oominakami Jinjya-Mitoyo01.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mitoyo City|Q873578|WD=Q873578|1=Mitoyo, Kagawa}} 2677-2 Hagata, Takase-cho |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deities = Oominakami Daimyojin, Homutawake no Mikoto, Munakata Okami |Founded = Unknown |Shrine Rank = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (Engishiki-listed small shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya#Ninomiya, Sannomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}, former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Annual Festival = |Rituals = |Main Hall = |Other Name = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Oominakami Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oominakami Shrine''' (おおみなかみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mitoyo City|Q873578|WD=Q873578|1=Mitoyo, Kagawa}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the Engishiki, and under the former {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is regarded as the Ninomiya of {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}}. === Overview === The goshuin (shrine stamp) reception desk is open on Saturdays and Sundays, or Sundays only, from around 1:30 PM to 4:00 PM; however, this may change depending on the month, so please check the Facebook page below in advance. During events such as the Great Purification Ceremony and kagura performances in early July, and outdoor taiko drum group live performances in October, the shrine grounds are illuminated with traditional Japanese umbrellas and bamboo lights. === History === The shrine began with the worship of two iwakura (sacred rock formations), one beside Unagi Fuchi behind the main hall and another behind Sen’go Ōji Shrine. Its founding is said to date back either to the Yayoi period or the Kofun period, and it is recorded in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō as the Ninomiya of Sanuki Province. Since ancient times, rainmaking rituals have been conducted here during times of drought. In the medieval period, the local lord Kondō Masamitsu invited Hachiman deity to the shrine. Together with Oominakami Daimyojin, Hachiman Ōkami and Mishima Ryūjin were enshrined, and it came to be known as “Ninomiya Sansha.” It became a shrine practicing syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, with Kiyozumi-dera and Jingū-ji serving as bettō temples, and prospered under the protection of the Kondō family. It is also said that Kūkai (Kōbō Daishi) visited the shrine before departing for Tang China, performed rituals, and composed dialogic waka poems with the three deities. Kūkai: “Having come from afar to worship, may you grant me the power of the three deities and protect me. In the early summer rains, with thoughts of the mountains, under the traveling sky I long for rest and come before you.” Reply from Hachiman Ōkami: “I am even now the god before the bow; know that I shall protect you even unto China.” Upper verse by Oominakami Daimyojin: “May your coming and going be at peace upon the sea of the sky,” Lower verse by Mishima Ryūjin: “I, the pure waters above, am the Dragon Deity.” From “Origins of Ninomiya Sansha” (compiled in the mid-Edo period) In 1184 (Genryaku 1), during the Genpei War, both the Minamoto and Taira clans dedicated written prayers to the shrine. In 1427 (Ōei 34), when the shrine was damaged, the emperor bore the cost of its reconstruction. It is said to have been burned by the Chōsokabe forces during the Sengoku period. The present buildings were rebuilt during the {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} era and renovated after the Meiji period. === Enshrined Deities === * Oominakami Daimyojin (sometimes identified with {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|1=Ōyamatsumi}}, though there are various theories) * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} * {{ill|lt=Munakata Three Goddesses|Q10948069|WD=Q10948069|lt=Munakata Ōkami|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}} === Shrine Grounds === [[File:Oominakami shrine 20240713 0.jpg|thumb|Main shrine worship hall]] * First Torii Gate: Located beside the prefectural road. * Suisen Bridge * Second Torii Gate: Beyond this lies a dense sacred area. * Misogi purification site * Shimenawa Stone: The sacred rope at this shrine is also straight across the worship hall. * Zuishin Gate * Third Torii Gate * Shigure Lantern: Prefecturally designated cultural property * Main Shrine Worship Hall and Main Hall * Ema Hall, Archery Range, Shrine Office * Unagi Fuchi (Ryūō Fuchi): Remains of rainmaking rituals. It is said that black and white {{ill|lt=eels|Q1422437|WD=Q1422437|1=Unagi}} live here. If a black eel appears during rainmaking, rain will fall; if a white eel appears, there will be drought; and if a {{ill|lt=crab|Q40802|WD=Q40802|1=Brachyura}} appears, strong winds will come.From explanatory signboards installed throughout the shrine grounds, and below as well. * Iwakura (Meoto Iwa): Ancient sacred objects central to Oominakami worship. A stone shrine stands atop the lower rock of a two-tiered formation. * Oominakami Mountain Stream Valley: A dark, densely wooded valley along the Miyagawa River, a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Saita River|Q11634632|WD=Q11634632|1=Saita River}}. * Haiku and Tanka Monuments: {{ill|lt=Mizuhara Shūōshi|Q1940999|WD=Q1940999|1=Shūōshi Mizuhara}} “Though not famed as a tea region, none compare to its fresh tea”; {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Seishi|Q2599126|WD=Q2599126|1=Yamaguchi Seishi}} “Fine is the new tea, enveloped in the mist of Oominakami.” Inscription of Kōbō Daishi’s prayer: “May your coming and going be peaceful upon the sea of the sky; I, the pure waters above, am the Dragon Deity.” From the Ninomiya records. === Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines === * Sen’go Ōji Shrine: Located up stone steps behind the main shrine grounds. A wooden shrine stands atop the lower rock beneath a three-tiered iwakura. Enshrines Izanagi no Mikoto and Izanami no Mikoto. ** Musubi Shrine: Enshrines the deity of binding (Musubi), connecting all prayers. * Shishamiya: Located beside the mikoshi storehouse on the left of the main grounds. Enshrines Taira no Norimori, Taira no Tsunemori, Taira no Sukemori, and Taira no Arimori. * Toyoha Shrine: Located on a hill to the left of the main grounds. Enshrines Aoba Ōkami, Kayano Hime no Mikoto, Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, and Sukunahikona no Mikoto. ** Ushigami Shrine: Enshrines Uke Mochi no Kami. ** Aramitama Shrine: Enshrines Ōmononushi no Kami. * Haraedo Shrine: Located to the right after passing through the Second Torii Gate. Enshrines Seoritsuhime no Kami, Hayakitsuhime no Kami, Ibukido Nushi no Kami, and Hayasasurahime no Kami. === Cultural Properties === ; National Historic Site * Ninomiya Kiln Ruins: Located at the mountain base outside the right side of the shrine grounds. Two kilns—No.1 (climbing kiln) on the right and No.2 (flat kiln) on the left—have yielded roof tiles, cups, and inkstones. A site spanning from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period. Designated April 25, 1932. ; Prefecturally Designated Tangible Cultural Property * Shigure Lantern: Total height 246 cm, made of tuff stone, created in 1346 (Kōei 4) by a craftsman of the highest rank “Hōkyō.” Designated April 9, 1963. ; Prefecturally Designated Natural Monument * Oominakami Shrine Juniper Tree: Located 20 meters north of the First Torii at the Otabisho. Height 12 m, branch spread 14 m, trunk circumference 4.4 m at breast height. Designated March 1976. === Access === From {{ill|lt=National Route 377|Q6157382|WD=Q6157382|1=Japan National Route 377}}, take {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefectural Road 218 Saita-Kamitakase Line|Q11667499|WD=Q11667499|1=Kagawa Prefectural Road Route 218}}. About 15 minutes from Sanuki-Toyonaka IC. Parking is available for 15 cars in front of the Zuishin Gate and 16 cars along the prefectural road in front of the First Torii. Entry to the shrine grounds is prohibited from 7:00 PM to around 5:00 AM due to the risk of wild boar sightings. === Notes === {{Notes help}} {{Reflist}} === External Links === {{Commonscat|Oominakami-jinja_(Mitoyo, Kagawa)}} * {{Instagram|minakaminomori}} * {{Facebook|ominakami}} * [Oominakami Shrine] - Mitoyo City {{Shinto navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:oominakamishinja}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oominakami Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oominakami Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Ni-no-Miya]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sanuki Province]] [[Category:香川県の神社]] [[Category:讃岐国の式内社]] [[Category:香川県の旧県社]] [[Category:三豊市の歴史]] [[Category:讃岐国|社2]] [[Category:三豊市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]]

Haiden (Worship Hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mitoyo City|Q873578|WD=Q873578|1=Mitoyo, Kagawa}} 2677-2 Hagata, Takase-cho |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deities = Oominakami Daimyojin, Homutawake no Mikoto, Munakata Okami |Founded = Unknown |Shrine Rank = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (Engishiki-listed small shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya#Ninomiya, Sannomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}, former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Annual Festival = |Rituals = |Main Hall = |Other Name = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Oominakami Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oominakami Shrine''' (おおみなかみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mitoyo City|Q873578|WD=Q873578|1=Mitoyo, Kagawa}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the Engishiki, and under the former {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is regarded as the Ninomiya of {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}}. === Overview === The goshuin (shrine stamp) reception desk is open on Saturdays and Sundays, or Sundays only, from around 1:30 PM to 4:00 PM; however, this may change depending on the month, so please check the Facebook page below in advance. During events such as the Great Purification Ceremony and kagura performances in early July, and outdoor taiko drum group live performances in October, the shrine grounds are illuminated with traditional Japanese umbrellas and bamboo lights. === History === The shrine began with the worship of two iwakura (sacred rock formations), one beside Unagi Fuchi behind the main hall and another behind Sen’go Ōji Shrine. Its founding is said to date back either to the Yayoi period or the Kofun period, and it is recorded in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō as the Ninomiya of Sanuki Province. Since ancient times, rainmaking rituals have been conducted here during times of drought. In the medieval period, the local lord Kondō Masamitsu invited Hachiman deity to the shrine. Together with Oominakami Daimyojin, Hachiman Ōkami and Mishima Ryūjin were enshrined, and it came to be known as “Ninomiya Sansha.” It became a shrine practicing syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, with Kiyozumi-dera and Jingū-ji serving as bettō temples, and prospered under the protection of the Kondō family. It is also said that Kūkai (Kōbō Daishi) visited the shrine before departing for Tang China, performed rituals, and composed dialogic waka poems with the three deities. Kūkai: “Having come from afar to worship, may you grant me the power of the three deities and protect me. In the early summer rains, with thoughts of the mountains, under the traveling sky I long for rest and come before you.” Reply from Hachiman Ōkami: “I am even now the god before the bow; know that I shall protect you even unto China.” Upper verse by Oominakami Daimyojin: “May your coming and going be at peace upon the sea of the sky,” Lower verse by Mishima Ryūjin: “I, the pure waters above, am the Dragon Deity.” From “Origins of Ninomiya Sansha” (compiled in the mid-Edo period) In 1184 (Genryaku 1), during the Genpei War, both the Minamoto and Taira clans dedicated written prayers to the shrine. In 1427 (Ōei 34), when the shrine was damaged, the emperor bore the cost of its reconstruction. It is said to have been burned by the Chōsokabe forces during the Sengoku period. The present buildings were rebuilt during the {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} era and renovated after the Meiji period. === Enshrined Deities === * Oominakami Daimyojin (sometimes identified with {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|1=Ōyamatsumi}}, though there are various theories) * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} * {{ill|lt=Munakata Three Goddesses|Q10948069|WD=Q10948069|lt=Munakata Ōkami|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}} === Shrine Grounds === [[File:Oominakami shrine 20240713 0.jpg|thumb|Main shrine worship hall]] * First Torii Gate: Located beside the prefectural road. * Suisen Bridge * Second Torii Gate: Beyond this lies a dense sacred area. * Misogi purification site * Shimenawa Stone: The sacred rope at this shrine is also straight across the worship hall. * Zuishin Gate * Third Torii Gate * Shigure Lantern: Prefecturally designated cultural property * Main Shrine Worship Hall and Main Hall * Ema Hall, Archery Range, Shrine Office * Unagi Fuchi (Ryūō Fuchi): Remains of rainmaking rituals. It is said that black and white {{ill|lt=eels|Q1422437|WD=Q1422437|1=Unagi}} live here. If a black eel appears during rainmaking, rain will fall; if a white eel appears, there will be drought; and if a {{ill|lt=crab|Q40802|WD=Q40802|1=Brachyura}} appears, strong winds will come.From explanatory signboards installed throughout the shrine grounds, and below as well. * Iwakura (Meoto Iwa): Ancient sacred objects central to Oominakami worship. A stone shrine stands atop the lower rock of a two-tiered formation. * Oominakami Mountain Stream Valley: A dark, densely wooded valley along the Miyagawa River, a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Saita River|Q11634632|WD=Q11634632|1=Saita River}}. * Haiku and Tanka Monuments: {{ill|lt=Mizuhara Shūōshi|Q1940999|WD=Q1940999|1=Shūōshi Mizuhara}} “Though not famed as a tea region, none compare to its fresh tea”; {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Seishi|Q2599126|WD=Q2599126|1=Yamaguchi Seishi}} “Fine is the new tea, enveloped in the mist of Oominakami.” Inscription of Kōbō Daishi’s prayer: “May your coming and going be peaceful upon the sea of the sky; I, the pure waters above, am the Dragon Deity.” From the Ninomiya records. === Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines === * Sen’go Ōji Shrine: Located up stone steps behind the main shrine grounds. A wooden shrine stands atop the lower rock beneath a three-tiered iwakura. Enshrines Izanagi no Mikoto and Izanami no Mikoto. ** Musubi Shrine: Enshrines the deity of binding (Musubi), connecting all prayers. * Shishamiya: Located beside the mikoshi storehouse on the left of the main grounds. Enshrines Taira no Norimori, Taira no Tsunemori, Taira no Sukemori, and Taira no Arimori. * Toyoha Shrine: Located on a hill to the left of the main grounds. Enshrines Aoba Ōkami, Kayano Hime no Mikoto, Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, and Sukunahikona no Mikoto. ** Ushigami Shrine: Enshrines Uke Mochi no Kami. ** Aramitama Shrine: Enshrines Ōmononushi no Kami. * Haraedo Shrine: Located to the right after passing through the Second Torii Gate. Enshrines Seoritsuhime no Kami, Hayakitsuhime no Kami, Ibukido Nushi no Kami, and Hayasasurahime no Kami. === Cultural Properties === ; National Historic Site * Ninomiya Kiln Ruins: Located at the mountain base outside the right side of the shrine grounds. Two kilns—No.1 (climbing kiln) on the right and No.2 (flat kiln) on the left—have yielded roof tiles, cups, and inkstones. A site spanning from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period. Designated April 25, 1932. ; Prefecturally Designated Tangible Cultural Property * Shigure Lantern: Total height 246 cm, made of tuff stone, created in 1346 (Kōei 4) by a craftsman of the highest rank “Hōkyō.” Designated April 9, 1963. ; Prefecturally Designated Natural Monument * Oominakami Shrine Juniper Tree: Located 20 meters north of the First Torii at the Otabisho. Height 12 m, branch spread 14 m, trunk circumference 4.4 m at breast height. Designated March 1976. === Access === From {{ill|lt=National Route 377|Q6157382|WD=Q6157382|1=Japan National Route 377}}, take {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefectural Road 218 Saita-Kamitakase Line|Q11667499|WD=Q11667499|1=Kagawa Prefectural Road Route 218}}. About 15 minutes from Sanuki-Toyonaka IC. Parking is available for 15 cars in front of the Zuishin Gate and 16 cars along the prefectural road in front of the First Torii. Entry to the shrine grounds is prohibited from 7:00 PM to around 5:00 AM due to the risk of wild boar sightings. === Notes === {{Notes help}} {{Reflist}} === External Links === {{Commonscat|Oominakami-jinja_(Mitoyo, Kagawa)}} * {{Instagram|minakaminomori}} * {{Facebook|ominakami}} * [Oominakami Shrine] - Mitoyo City {{Shinto navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:oominakamishinja}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oominakami Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oominakami Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Ni-no-Miya]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sanuki Province]] [[Category:香川県の神社]] [[Category:讃岐国の式内社]] [[Category:香川県の旧県社]] [[Category:三豊市の歴史]] [[Category:讃岐国|社2]] [[Category:三豊市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]]

Haiden (Worship Hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mitoyo City|Q873578|WD=Q873578|1=Mitoyo, Kagawa}} 2677-2 Hagata, Takase-cho |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deities = Oominakami Daimyojin, Homutawake no Mikoto, Munakata Okami |Founded = Unknown |Shrine Rank = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (Engishiki-listed small shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya#Ninomiya, Sannomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}, former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Annual Festival = |Rituals = |Main Hall = |Other Name = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Oominakami Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oominakami Shrine''' (おおみなかみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mitoyo City|Q873578|WD=Q873578|1=Mitoyo, Kagawa}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the Engishiki, and under the former {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is regarded as the Ninomiya of {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}}. === Overview === The goshuin (shrine stamp) reception desk is open on Saturdays and Sundays, or Sundays only, from around 1:30 PM to 4:00 PM; however, this may change depending on the month, so please check the Facebook page below in advance. During events such as the Great Purification Ceremony and kagura performances in early July, and outdoor taiko drum group live performances in October, the shrine grounds are illuminated with traditional Japanese umbrellas and bamboo lights. === History === The shrine began with the worship of two iwakura (sacred rock formations), one beside Unagi Fuchi behind the main hall and another behind Sen’go Ōji Shrine. Its founding is said to date back either to the Yayoi period or the Kofun period, and it is recorded in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō as the Ninomiya of Sanuki Province. Since ancient times, rainmaking rituals have been conducted here during times of drought. In the medieval period, the local lord Kondō Masamitsu invited Hachiman deity to the shrine. Together with Oominakami Daimyojin, Hachiman Ōkami and Mishima Ryūjin were enshrined, and it came to be known as “Ninomiya Sansha.” It became a shrine practicing syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, with Kiyozumi-dera and Jingū-ji serving as bettō temples, and prospered under the protection of the Kondō family. It is also said that Kūkai (Kōbō Daishi) visited the shrine before departing for Tang China, performed rituals, and composed dialogic waka poems with the three deities. Kūkai: “Having come from afar to worship, may you grant me the power of the three deities and protect me. In the early summer rains, with thoughts of the mountains, under the traveling sky I long for rest and come before you.” Reply from Hachiman Ōkami: “I am even now the god before the bow; know that I shall protect you even unto China.” Upper verse by Oominakami Daimyojin: “May your coming and going be at peace upon the sea of the sky,” Lower verse by Mishima Ryūjin: “I, the pure waters above, am the Dragon Deity.” From “Origins of Ninomiya Sansha” (compiled in the mid-Edo period) In 1184 (Genryaku 1), during the Genpei War, both the Minamoto and Taira clans dedicated written prayers to the shrine. In 1427 (Ōei 34), when the shrine was damaged, the emperor bore the cost of its reconstruction. It is said to have been burned by the Chōsokabe forces during the Sengoku period. The present buildings were rebuilt during the {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} era and renovated after the Meiji period. === Enshrined Deities === * Oominakami Daimyojin (sometimes identified with {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|1=Ōyamatsumi}}, though there are various theories) * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} * {{ill|lt=Munakata Three Goddesses|Q10948069|WD=Q10948069|lt=Munakata Ōkami|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}} === Shrine Grounds === [[File:Oominakami shrine 20240713 0.jpg|thumb|Main shrine worship hall]] * First Torii Gate: Located beside the prefectural road. * Suisen Bridge * Second Torii Gate: Beyond this lies a dense sacred area. * Misogi purification site * Shimenawa Stone: The sacred rope at this shrine is also straight across the worship hall. * Zuishin Gate * Third Torii Gate * Shigure Lantern: Prefecturally designated cultural property * Main Shrine Worship Hall and Main Hall * Ema Hall, Archery Range, Shrine Office * Unagi Fuchi (Ryūō Fuchi): Remains of rainmaking rituals. It is said that black and white {{ill|lt=eels|Q1422437|WD=Q1422437|1=Unagi}} live here. If a black eel appears during rainmaking, rain will fall; if a white eel appears, there will be drought; and if a {{ill|lt=crab|Q40802|WD=Q40802|1=Brachyura}} appears, strong winds will come.From explanatory signboards installed throughout the shrine grounds, and below as well. * Iwakura (Meoto Iwa): Ancient sacred objects central to Oominakami worship. A stone shrine stands atop the lower rock of a two-tiered formation. * Oominakami Mountain Stream Valley: A dark, densely wooded valley along the Miyagawa River, a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Saita River|Q11634632|WD=Q11634632|1=Saita River}}. * Haiku and Tanka Monuments: {{ill|lt=Mizuhara Shūōshi|Q1940999|WD=Q1940999|1=Shūōshi Mizuhara}} “Though not famed as a tea region, none compare to its fresh tea”; {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Seishi|Q2599126|WD=Q2599126|1=Yamaguchi Seishi}} “Fine is the new tea, enveloped in the mist of Oominakami.” Inscription of Kōbō Daishi’s prayer: “May your coming and going be peaceful upon the sea of the sky; I, the pure waters above, am the Dragon Deity.” From the Ninomiya records. === Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines === * Sen’go Ōji Shrine: Located up stone steps behind the main shrine grounds. A wooden shrine stands atop the lower rock beneath a three-tiered iwakura. Enshrines Izanagi no Mikoto and Izanami no Mikoto. ** Musubi Shrine: Enshrines the deity of binding (Musubi), connecting all prayers. * Shishamiya: Located beside the mikoshi storehouse on the left of the main grounds. Enshrines Taira no Norimori, Taira no Tsunemori, Taira no Sukemori, and Taira no Arimori. * Toyoha Shrine: Located on a hill to the left of the main grounds. Enshrines Aoba Ōkami, Kayano Hime no Mikoto, Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, and Sukunahikona no Mikoto. ** Ushigami Shrine: Enshrines Uke Mochi no Kami. ** Aramitama Shrine: Enshrines Ōmononushi no Kami. * Haraedo Shrine: Located to the right after passing through the Second Torii Gate. Enshrines Seoritsuhime no Kami, Hayakitsuhime no Kami, Ibukido Nushi no Kami, and Hayasasurahime no Kami. === Cultural Properties === ; National Historic Site * Ninomiya Kiln Ruins: Located at the mountain base outside the right side of the shrine grounds. Two kilns—No.1 (climbing kiln) on the right and No.2 (flat kiln) on the left—have yielded roof tiles, cups, and inkstones. A site spanning from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period. Designated April 25, 1932. ; Prefecturally Designated Tangible Cultural Property * Shigure Lantern: Total height 246 cm, made of tuff stone, created in 1346 (Kōei 4) by a craftsman of the highest rank “Hōkyō.” Designated April 9, 1963. ; Prefecturally Designated Natural Monument * Oominakami Shrine Juniper Tree: Located 20 meters north of the First Torii at the Otabisho. Height 12 m, branch spread 14 m, trunk circumference 4.4 m at breast height. Designated March 1976. === Access === From {{ill|lt=National Route 377|Q6157382|WD=Q6157382|1=Japan National Route 377}}, take {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefectural Road 218 Saita-Kamitakase Line|Q11667499|WD=Q11667499|1=Kagawa Prefectural Road Route 218}}. About 15 minutes from Sanuki-Toyonaka IC. Parking is available for 15 cars in front of the Zuishin Gate and 16 cars along the prefectural road in front of the First Torii. Entry to the shrine grounds is prohibited from 7:00 PM to around 5:00 AM due to the risk of wild boar sightings. === Notes === {{Notes help}} {{Reflist}} === External Links === {{Commonscat|Oominakami-jinja_(Mitoyo, Kagawa)}} * {{Instagram|minakaminomori}} * {{Facebook|ominakami}} * [Oominakami Shrine] - Mitoyo City {{Shinto navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oominakami Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oominakami Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Ni-no-Miya]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sanuki Province]] [[Category:香川県の神社]] [[Category:讃岐国の式内社]] [[Category:香川県の旧県社]] [[Category:三豊市の歴史]] [[Category:讃岐国|社2]] [[Category:三豊市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]]

category|Category:Oominakami-jinja (Mitoyo, Kagawa)}} [[JA:大水上神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11436783}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Oominakami Shrine |Image = [[Image:Oominakami Jinjya-Mitoyo01.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mitoyo City|Q873578|WD=Q873578|1=Mitoyo, Kagawa}} 2677-2 Hagata, Takase-cho |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deities = Oominakami Daimyojin, Homutawake no Mikoto, Munakata Okami |Founded = Unknown |Shrine Rank = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (Engishiki-listed small shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya#Ninomiya, Sannomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}, former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Annual Festival = |Rituals = |Main Hall = |Other Name = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Oominakami Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oominakami Shrine''' (おおみなかみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mitoyo City|Q873578|WD=Q873578|1=Mitoyo, Kagawa}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the Engishiki, and under the former {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is regarded as the Ninomiya of {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}}. === Overview === The goshuin (shrine stamp) reception desk is open on Saturdays and Sundays, or Sundays only, from around 1:30 PM to 4:00 PM; however, this may change depending on the month, so please check the Facebook page below in advance. During events such as the Great Purification Ceremony and kagura performances in early July, and outdoor taiko drum group live performances in October, the shrine grounds are illuminated with traditional Japanese umbrellas and bamboo lights. === History === The shrine began with the worship of two iwakura (sacred rock formations), one beside Unagi Fuchi behind the main hall and another behind Sen’go Ōji Shrine. Its founding is said to date back either to the Yayoi period or the Kofun period, and it is recorded in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō as the Ninomiya of Sanuki Province. Since ancient times, rainmaking rituals have been conducted here during times of drought. In the medieval period, the local lord Kondō Masamitsu invited Hachiman deity to the shrine. Together with Oominakami Daimyojin, Hachiman Ōkami and Mishima Ryūjin were enshrined, and it came to be known as “Ninomiya Sansha.” It became a shrine practicing syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, with Kiyozumi-dera and Jingū-ji serving as bettō temples, and prospered under the protection of the Kondō family. It is also said that Kūkai (Kōbō Daishi) visited the shrine before departing for Tang China, performed rituals, and composed dialogic waka poems with the three deities. Kūkai: “Having come from afar to worship, may you grant me the power of the three deities and protect me. In the early summer rains, with thoughts of the mountains, under the traveling sky I long for rest and come before you.” Reply from Hachiman Ōkami: “I am even now the god before the bow; know that I shall protect you even unto China.” Upper verse by Oominakami Daimyojin: “May your coming and going be at peace upon the sea of the sky,” Lower verse by Mishima Ryūjin: “I, the pure waters above, am the Dragon Deity.” From “Origins of Ninomiya Sansha” (compiled in the mid-Edo period) In 1184 (Genryaku 1), during the Genpei War, both the Minamoto and Taira clans dedicated written prayers to the shrine. In 1427 (Ōei 34), when the shrine was damaged, the emperor bore the cost of its reconstruction. It is said to have been burned by the Chōsokabe forces during the Sengoku period. The present buildings were rebuilt during the {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} era and renovated after the Meiji period. === Enshrined Deities === * Oominakami Daimyojin (sometimes identified with {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|1=Ōyamatsumi}}, though there are various theories) * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} * {{ill|lt=Munakata Three Goddesses|Q10948069|WD=Q10948069|lt=Munakata Ōkami|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}} === Shrine Grounds === [[File:Oominakami shrine 20240713 0.jpg|thumb|Main shrine worship hall]] * First Torii Gate: Located beside the prefectural road. * Suisen Bridge * Second Torii Gate: Beyond this lies a dense sacred area. * Misogi purification site * Shimenawa Stone: The sacred rope at this shrine is also straight across the worship hall. * Zuishin Gate * Third Torii Gate * Shigure Lantern: Prefecturally designated cultural property * Main Shrine Worship Hall and Main Hall * Ema Hall, Archery Range, Shrine Office * Unagi Fuchi (Ryūō Fuchi): Remains of rainmaking rituals. It is said that black and white {{ill|lt=eels|Q1422437|WD=Q1422437|1=Unagi}} live here. If a black eel appears during rainmaking, rain will fall; if a white eel appears, there will be drought; and if a {{ill|lt=crab|Q40802|WD=Q40802|1=Brachyura}} appears, strong winds will come.From explanatory signboards installed throughout the shrine grounds, and below as well. * Iwakura (Meoto Iwa): Ancient sacred objects central to Oominakami worship. A stone shrine stands atop the lower rock of a two-tiered formation. * Oominakami Mountain Stream Valley: A dark, densely wooded valley along the Miyagawa River, a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Saita River|Q11634632|WD=Q11634632|1=Saita River}}. * Haiku and Tanka Monuments: {{ill|lt=Mizuhara Shūōshi|Q1940999|WD=Q1940999|1=Shūōshi Mizuhara}} “Though not famed as a tea region, none compare to its fresh tea”; {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Seishi|Q2599126|WD=Q2599126|1=Yamaguchi Seishi}} “Fine is the new tea, enveloped in the mist of Oominakami.” Inscription of Kōbō Daishi’s prayer: “May your coming and going be peaceful upon the sea of the sky; I, the pure waters above, am the Dragon Deity.” From the Ninomiya records. === Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines === * Sen’go Ōji Shrine: Located up stone steps behind the main shrine grounds. A wooden shrine stands atop the lower rock beneath a three-tiered iwakura. Enshrines Izanagi no Mikoto and Izanami no Mikoto. ** Musubi Shrine: Enshrines the deity of binding (Musubi), connecting all prayers. * Shishamiya: Located beside the mikoshi storehouse on the left of the main grounds. Enshrines Taira no Norimori, Taira no Tsunemori, Taira no Sukemori, and Taira no Arimori. * Toyoha Shrine: Located on a hill to the left of the main grounds. Enshrines Aoba Ōkami, Kayano Hime no Mikoto, Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, and Sukunahikona no Mikoto. ** Ushigami Shrine: Enshrines Uke Mochi no Kami. ** Aramitama Shrine: Enshrines Ōmononushi no Kami. * Haraedo Shrine: Located to the right after passing through the Second Torii Gate. Enshrines Seoritsuhime no Kami, Hayakitsuhime no Kami, Ibukido Nushi no Kami, and Hayasasurahime no Kami. === Cultural Properties === ; National Historic Site * Ninomiya Kiln Ruins: Located at the mountain base outside the right side of the shrine grounds. Two kilns—No.1 (climbing kiln) on the right and No.2 (flat kiln) on the left—have yielded roof tiles, cups, and inkstones. A site spanning from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period. Designated April 25, 1932. ; Prefecturally Designated Tangible Cultural Property * Shigure Lantern: Total height 246 cm, made of tuff stone, created in 1346 (Kōei 4) by a craftsman of the highest rank “Hōkyō.” Designated April 9, 1963. ; Prefecturally Designated Natural Monument * Oominakami Shrine Juniper Tree: Located 20 meters north of the First Torii at the Otabisho. Height 12 m, branch spread 14 m, trunk circumference 4.4 m at breast height. Designated March 1976. === Access === From {{ill|lt=National Route 377|Q6157382|WD=Q6157382|1=Japan National Route 377}}, take {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefectural Road 218 Saita-Kamitakase Line|Q11667499|WD=Q11667499|1=Kagawa Prefectural Road Route 218}}. About 15 minutes from Sanuki-Toyonaka IC. Parking is available for 15 cars in front of the Zuishin Gate and 16 cars along the prefectural road in front of the First Torii. Entry to the shrine grounds is prohibited from 7:00 PM to around 5:00 AM due to the risk of wild boar sightings. === Notes === {{Notes help}} {{Reflist}} === External Links === {{Commonscat|Oominakami-jinja_(Mitoyo, Kagawa)}} * {{Instagram|minakaminomori}} * {{Facebook|ominakami}} * [Oominakami Shrine] - Mitoyo City {{Shinto navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oominakami Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oominakami Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Ni-no-Miya]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sanuki Province]] [[Category:香川県の神社]] [[Category:讃岐国の式内社]] [[Category:香川県の旧県社]] [[Category:三豊市の歴史]] [[Category:讃岐国|社2]] [[Category:Buildings in Mitoyo, Kagawa]] [[Category:pages]]

category|Category:Oominakami-jinja (Mitoyo, Kagawa)}} [[JA:大水上神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11436783}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Oominakami Shrine |Image = [[Image:Oominakami Jinjya-Mitoyo01.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mitoyo City|Q873578|WD=Q873578|1=Mitoyo, Kagawa}} 2677-2 Hagata, Takase-cho |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deities = Oominakami Daimyojin, Homutawake no Mikoto, Munakata Okami |Founded = Unknown |Shrine Rank = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (Engishiki-listed small shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya#Ninomiya, Sannomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}, former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Annual Festival = |Rituals = |Main Hall = |Other Name = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Oominakami Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oominakami Shrine''' (おおみなかみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mitoyo City|Q873578|WD=Q873578|1=Mitoyo, Kagawa}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the Engishiki, and under the former {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is regarded as the Ninomiya of {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}}. === Overview === The goshuin (shrine stamp) reception desk is open on Saturdays and Sundays, or Sundays only, from around 1:30 PM to 4:00 PM; however, this may change depending on the month, so please check the Facebook page below in advance. During events such as the Great Purification Ceremony and kagura performances in early July, and outdoor taiko drum group live performances in October, the shrine grounds are illuminated with traditional Japanese umbrellas and bamboo lights. === History === The shrine began with the worship of two iwakura (sacred rock formations), one beside Unagi Fuchi behind the main hall and another behind Sen’go Ōji Shrine. Its founding is said to date back either to the Yayoi period or the Kofun period, and it is recorded in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō as the Ninomiya of Sanuki Province. Since ancient times, rainmaking rituals have been conducted here during times of drought. In the medieval period, the local lord Kondō Masamitsu invited Hachiman deity to the shrine. Together with Oominakami Daimyojin, Hachiman Ōkami and Mishima Ryūjin were enshrined, and it came to be known as “Ninomiya Sansha.” It became a shrine practicing syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, with Kiyozumi-dera and Jingū-ji serving as bettō temples, and prospered under the protection of the Kondō family. It is also said that Kūkai (Kōbō Daishi) visited the shrine before departing for Tang China, performed rituals, and composed dialogic waka poems with the three deities. Kūkai: “Having come from afar to worship, may you grant me the power of the three deities and protect me. In the early summer rains, with thoughts of the mountains, under the traveling sky I long for rest and come before you.” Reply from Hachiman Ōkami: “I am even now the god before the bow; know that I shall protect you even unto China.” Upper verse by Oominakami Daimyojin: “May your coming and going be at peace upon the sea of the sky,” Lower verse by Mishima Ryūjin: “I, the pure waters above, am the Dragon Deity.” From “Origins of Ninomiya Sansha” (compiled in the mid-Edo period) In 1184 (Genryaku 1), during the Genpei War, both the Minamoto and Taira clans dedicated written prayers to the shrine. In 1427 (Ōei 34), when the shrine was damaged, the emperor bore the cost of its reconstruction. It is said to have been burned by the Chōsokabe forces during the Sengoku period. The present buildings were rebuilt during the {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} era and renovated after the Meiji period. === Enshrined Deities === * Oominakami Daimyojin (sometimes identified with {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|1=Ōyamatsumi}}, though there are various theories) * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} * {{ill|lt=Munakata Three Goddesses|Q10948069|WD=Q10948069|lt=Munakata Ōkami|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}} === Shrine Grounds === [[File:Oominakami shrine 20240713 0.jpg|thumb|Main shrine worship hall]] * First Torii Gate: Located beside the prefectural road. * Suisen Bridge * Second Torii Gate: Beyond this lies a dense sacred area. * Misogi purification site * Shimenawa Stone: The sacred rope at this shrine is also straight across the worship hall. * Zuishin Gate * Third Torii Gate * Shigure Lantern: Prefecturally designated cultural property * Main Shrine Worship Hall and Main Hall * Ema Hall, Archery Range, Shrine Office * Unagi Fuchi (Ryūō Fuchi): Remains of rainmaking rituals. It is said that black and white {{ill|lt=eels|Q1422437|WD=Q1422437|1=Unagi}} live here. If a black eel appears during rainmaking, rain will fall; if a white eel appears, there will be drought; and if a {{ill|lt=crab|Q40802|WD=Q40802|1=Brachyura}} appears, strong winds will come.From explanatory signboards installed throughout the shrine grounds, and below as well. * Iwakura (Meoto Iwa): Ancient sacred objects central to Oominakami worship. A stone shrine stands atop the lower rock of a two-tiered formation. * Oominakami Mountain Stream Valley: A dark, densely wooded valley along the Miyagawa River, a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Saita River|Q11634632|WD=Q11634632|1=Saita River}}. * Haiku and Tanka Monuments: {{ill|lt=Mizuhara Shūōshi|Q1940999|WD=Q1940999|1=Shūōshi Mizuhara}} “Though not famed as a tea region, none compare to its fresh tea”; {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Seishi|Q2599126|WD=Q2599126|1=Yamaguchi Seishi}} “Fine is the new tea, enveloped in the mist of Oominakami.” Inscription of Kōbō Daishi’s prayer: “May your coming and going be peaceful upon the sea of the sky; I, the pure waters above, am the Dragon Deity.” From the Ninomiya records. === Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines === * Sen’go Ōji Shrine: Located up stone steps behind the main shrine grounds. A wooden shrine stands atop the lower rock beneath a three-tiered iwakura. Enshrines Izanagi no Mikoto and Izanami no Mikoto. ** Musubi Shrine: Enshrines the deity of binding (Musubi), connecting all prayers. * Shishamiya: Located beside the mikoshi storehouse on the left of the main grounds. Enshrines Taira no Norimori, Taira no Tsunemori, Taira no Sukemori, and Taira no Arimori. * Toyoha Shrine: Located on a hill to the left of the main grounds. Enshrines Aoba Ōkami, Kayano Hime no Mikoto, Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, and Sukunahikona no Mikoto. ** Ushigami Shrine: Enshrines Uke Mochi no Kami. ** Aramitama Shrine: Enshrines Ōmononushi no Kami. * Haraedo Shrine: Located to the right after passing through the Second Torii Gate. Enshrines Seoritsuhime no Kami, Hayakitsuhime no Kami, Ibukido Nushi no Kami, and Hayasasurahime no Kami. === Cultural Properties === ; National Historic Site * Ninomiya Kiln Ruins: Located at the mountain base outside the right side of the shrine grounds. Two kilns—No.1 (climbing kiln) on the right and No.2 (flat kiln) on the left—have yielded roof tiles, cups, and inkstones. A site spanning from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period. Designated April 25, 1932. ; Prefecturally Designated Tangible Cultural Property * Shigure Lantern: Total height 246 cm, made of tuff stone, created in 1346 (Kōei 4) by a craftsman of the highest rank “Hōkyō.” Designated April 9, 1963. ; Prefecturally Designated Natural Monument * Oominakami Shrine Juniper Tree: Located 20 meters north of the First Torii at the Otabisho. Height 12 m, branch spread 14 m, trunk circumference 4.4 m at breast height. Designated March 1976. === Access === From {{ill|lt=National Route 377|Q6157382|WD=Q6157382|1=Japan National Route 377}}, take {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefectural Road 218 Saita-Kamitakase Line|Q11667499|WD=Q11667499|1=Kagawa Prefectural Road Route 218}}. About 15 minutes from Sanuki-Toyonaka IC. Parking is available for 15 cars in front of the Zuishin Gate and 16 cars along the prefectural road in front of the First Torii. Entry to the shrine grounds is prohibited from 7:00 PM to around 5:00 AM due to the risk of wild boar sightings. === Notes === {{Notes help}} {{Reflist}} === External Links === {{Commonscat|Oominakami-jinja_(Mitoyo, Kagawa)}} * {{Instagram|minakaminomori}} * {{Facebook|ominakami}} * [Oominakami Shrine] - Mitoyo City {{Shinto navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oominakami Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oominakami Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Ni-no-Miya]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sanuki Province]] [[Category:香川県の神社]] [[Category:讃岐国の式内社]] [[Category:香川県の旧県社]] [[Category:Municipal History of Mitoyo]] [[Category:讃岐国|社2]] [[Category:Buildings in Mitoyo, Kagawa]] [[Category:pages]]

category|Category:Oominakami-jinja (Mitoyo, Kagawa)}} [[JA:大水上神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11436783}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Oominakami Shrine |Image = [[Image:Oominakami Jinjya-Mitoyo01.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mitoyo City|Q873578|WD=Q873578|1=Mitoyo, Kagawa}} 2677-2 Hagata, Takase-cho |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deities = Oominakami Daimyojin, Homutawake no Mikoto, Munakata Okami |Founded = Unknown |Shrine Rank = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (Engishiki-listed small shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya#Ninomiya, Sannomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}, former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Annual Festival = |Rituals = |Main Hall = |Other Name = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Oominakami Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oominakami Shrine''' (おおみなかみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mitoyo City|Q873578|WD=Q873578|1=Mitoyo, Kagawa}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the Engishiki, and under the former {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is regarded as the Ninomiya of {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}}. === Overview === The goshuin (shrine stamp) reception desk is open on Saturdays and Sundays, or Sundays only, from around 1:30 PM to 4:00 PM; however, this may change depending on the month, so please check the Facebook page below in advance. During events such as the Great Purification Ceremony and kagura performances in early July, and outdoor taiko drum group live performances in October, the shrine grounds are illuminated with traditional Japanese umbrellas and bamboo lights. === History === The shrine began with the worship of two iwakura (sacred rock formations), one beside Unagi Fuchi behind the main hall and another behind Sen’go Ōji Shrine. Its founding is said to date back either to the Yayoi period or the Kofun period, and it is recorded in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō as the Ninomiya of Sanuki Province. Since ancient times, rainmaking rituals have been conducted here during times of drought. In the medieval period, the local lord Kondō Masamitsu invited Hachiman deity to the shrine. Together with Oominakami Daimyojin, Hachiman Ōkami and Mishima Ryūjin were enshrined, and it came to be known as “Ninomiya Sansha.” It became a shrine practicing syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, with Kiyozumi-dera and Jingū-ji serving as bettō temples, and prospered under the protection of the Kondō family. It is also said that Kūkai (Kōbō Daishi) visited the shrine before departing for Tang China, performed rituals, and composed dialogic waka poems with the three deities. Kūkai: “Having come from afar to worship, may you grant me the power of the three deities and protect me. In the early summer rains, with thoughts of the mountains, under the traveling sky I long for rest and come before you.” Reply from Hachiman Ōkami: “I am even now the god before the bow; know that I shall protect you even unto China.” Upper verse by Oominakami Daimyojin: “May your coming and going be at peace upon the sea of the sky,” Lower verse by Mishima Ryūjin: “I, the pure waters above, am the Dragon Deity.” From “Origins of Ninomiya Sansha” (compiled in the mid-Edo period) In 1184 (Genryaku 1), during the Genpei War, both the Minamoto and Taira clans dedicated written prayers to the shrine. In 1427 (Ōei 34), when the shrine was damaged, the emperor bore the cost of its reconstruction. It is said to have been burned by the Chōsokabe forces during the Sengoku period. The present buildings were rebuilt during the {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} era and renovated after the Meiji period. === Enshrined Deities === * Oominakami Daimyojin (sometimes identified with {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|1=Ōyamatsumi}}, though there are various theories) * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} * {{ill|lt=Munakata Three Goddesses|Q10948069|WD=Q10948069|lt=Munakata Ōkami|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}} === Shrine Grounds === [[File:Oominakami shrine 20240713 0.jpg|thumb|Main shrine worship hall]] * First Torii Gate: Located beside the prefectural road. * Suisen Bridge * Second Torii Gate: Beyond this lies a dense sacred area. * Misogi purification site * Shimenawa Stone: The sacred rope at this shrine is also straight across the worship hall. * Zuishin Gate * Third Torii Gate * Shigure Lantern: Prefecturally designated cultural property * Main Shrine Worship Hall and Main Hall * Ema Hall, Archery Range, Shrine Office * Unagi Fuchi (Ryūō Fuchi): Remains of rainmaking rituals. It is said that black and white {{ill|lt=eels|Q1422437|WD=Q1422437|1=Unagi}} live here. If a black eel appears during rainmaking, rain will fall; if a white eel appears, there will be drought; and if a {{ill|lt=crab|Q40802|WD=Q40802|1=Brachyura}} appears, strong winds will come.From explanatory signboards installed throughout the shrine grounds, and below as well. * Iwakura (Meoto Iwa): Ancient sacred objects central to Oominakami worship. A stone shrine stands atop the lower rock of a two-tiered formation. * Oominakami Mountain Stream Valley: A dark, densely wooded valley along the Miyagawa River, a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Saita River|Q11634632|WD=Q11634632|1=Saita River}}. * Haiku and Tanka Monuments: {{ill|lt=Mizuhara Shūōshi|Q1940999|WD=Q1940999|1=Shūōshi Mizuhara}} “Though not famed as a tea region, none compare to its fresh tea”; {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Seishi|Q2599126|WD=Q2599126|1=Yamaguchi Seishi}} “Fine is the new tea, enveloped in the mist of Oominakami.” Inscription of Kōbō Daishi’s prayer: “May your coming and going be peaceful upon the sea of the sky; I, the pure waters above, am the Dragon Deity.” From the Ninomiya records. === Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines === * Sen’go Ōji Shrine: Located up stone steps behind the main shrine grounds. A wooden shrine stands atop the lower rock beneath a three-tiered iwakura. Enshrines Izanagi no Mikoto and Izanami no Mikoto. ** Musubi Shrine: Enshrines the deity of binding (Musubi), connecting all prayers. * Shishamiya: Located beside the mikoshi storehouse on the left of the main grounds. Enshrines Taira no Norimori, Taira no Tsunemori, Taira no Sukemori, and Taira no Arimori. * Toyoha Shrine: Located on a hill to the left of the main grounds. Enshrines Aoba Ōkami, Kayano Hime no Mikoto, Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, and Sukunahikona no Mikoto. ** Ushigami Shrine: Enshrines Uke Mochi no Kami. ** Aramitama Shrine: Enshrines Ōmononushi no Kami. * Haraedo Shrine: Located to the right after passing through the Second Torii Gate. Enshrines Seoritsuhime no Kami, Hayakitsuhime no Kami, Ibukido Nushi no Kami, and Hayasasurahime no Kami. === Cultural Properties === ; National Historic Site * Ninomiya Kiln Ruins: Located at the mountain base outside the right side of the shrine grounds. Two kilns—No.1 (climbing kiln) on the right and No.2 (flat kiln) on the left—have yielded roof tiles, cups, and inkstones. A site spanning from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period. Designated April 25, 1932. ; Prefecturally Designated Tangible Cultural Property * Shigure Lantern: Total height 246 cm, made of tuff stone, created in 1346 (Kōei 4) by a craftsman of the highest rank “Hōkyō.” Designated April 9, 1963. ; Prefecturally Designated Natural Monument * Oominakami Shrine Juniper Tree: Located 20 meters north of the First Torii at the Otabisho. Height 12 m, branch spread 14 m, trunk circumference 4.4 m at breast height. Designated March 1976. === Access === From {{ill|lt=National Route 377|Q6157382|WD=Q6157382|1=Japan National Route 377}}, take {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefectural Road 218 Saita-Kamitakase Line|Q11667499|WD=Q11667499|1=Kagawa Prefectural Road Route 218}}. About 15 minutes from Sanuki-Toyonaka IC. Parking is available for 15 cars in front of the Zuishin Gate and 16 cars along the prefectural road in front of the First Torii. Entry to the shrine grounds is prohibited from 7:00 PM to around 5:00 AM due to the risk of wild boar sightings. === Notes === {{Notes help}} {{Reflist}} === External Links === {{Commonscat|Oominakami-jinja_(Mitoyo, Kagawa)}} * {{Instagram|minakaminomori}} * {{Facebook|ominakami}} * [Oominakami Shrine] - Mitoyo City {{Shinto navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oominakami Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oominakami Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Ni-no-Miya]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sanuki Province]] [[Category:香川県の神社]] [[Category:讃岐国の式内社]] [[Category:香川県の旧県社]] [[Category:Municipal History of Mitoyo]] [[Category:Sanuki Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Mitoyo, Kagawa]] [[Category:pages]]

Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 2470005003981 == Commons category (P373) == * Oominakami-jinja (Mitoyo, Kagawa) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '香川県三豊市高瀬町羽方2677番地2', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Oominakami-jinja (Mitoyo, Kagawa)}} [[JA:大水上神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11436783}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Oominakami Shrine |Image = [[Image:Oominakami Jinjya-Mitoyo01.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mitoyo City|Q873578|WD=Q873578|1=Mitoyo, Kagawa}} 2677-2 Hagata, Takase-cho |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deities = Oominakami Daimyojin, Homutawake no Mikoto, Munakata Okami |Founded = Unknown |Shrine Rank = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (Engishiki-listed small shrine)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya#Ninomiya, Sannomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Ninomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}, former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Annual Festival = |Rituals = |Main Hall = |Other Name = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Oominakami Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Oominakami Shrine''' (おおみなかみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mitoyo City|Q873578|WD=Q873578|1=Mitoyo, Kagawa}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the Engishiki, and under the former {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It is regarded as the Ninomiya of {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}}. === Overview === The goshuin (shrine stamp) reception desk is open on Saturdays and Sundays, or Sundays only, from around 1:30 PM to 4:00 PM; however, this may change depending on the month, so please check the Facebook page below in advance. During events such as the Great Purification Ceremony and kagura performances in early July, and outdoor taiko drum group live performances in October, the shrine grounds are illuminated with traditional Japanese umbrellas and bamboo lights. === History === The shrine began with the worship of two iwakura (sacred rock formations), one beside Unagi Fuchi behind the main hall and another behind Sen’go Ōji Shrine. Its founding is said to date back either to the Yayoi period or the Kofun period, and it is recorded in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō as the Ninomiya of Sanuki Province. Since ancient times, rainmaking rituals have been conducted here during times of drought. In the medieval period, the local lord Kondō Masamitsu invited Hachiman deity to the shrine. Together with Oominakami Daimyojin, Hachiman Ōkami and Mishima Ryūjin were enshrined, and it came to be known as “Ninomiya Sansha.” It became a shrine practicing syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, with Kiyozumi-dera and Jingū-ji serving as bettō temples, and prospered under the protection of the Kondō family. It is also said that Kūkai (Kōbō Daishi) visited the shrine before departing for Tang China, performed rituals, and composed dialogic waka poems with the three deities. Kūkai: “Having come from afar to worship, may you grant me the power of the three deities and protect me. In the early summer rains, with thoughts of the mountains, under the traveling sky I long for rest and come before you.” Reply from Hachiman Ōkami: “I am even now the god before the bow; know that I shall protect you even unto China.” Upper verse by Oominakami Daimyojin: “May your coming and going be at peace upon the sea of the sky,” Lower verse by Mishima Ryūjin: “I, the pure waters above, am the Dragon Deity.” From “Origins of Ninomiya Sansha” (compiled in the mid-Edo period) In 1184 (Genryaku 1), during the Genpei War, both the Minamoto and Taira clans dedicated written prayers to the shrine. In 1427 (Ōei 34), when the shrine was damaged, the emperor bore the cost of its reconstruction. It is said to have been burned by the Chōsokabe forces during the Sengoku period. The present buildings were rebuilt during the {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|WD=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} era and renovated after the Meiji period. === Enshrined Deities === * Oominakami Daimyojin (sometimes identified with {{ill|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi|Q386563|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamazumi no Mikoto|1=Ōyamatsumi}}, though there are various theories) * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Homutawake no Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} * {{ill|lt=Munakata Three Goddesses|Q10948069|WD=Q10948069|lt=Munakata Ōkami|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}} === Shrine Grounds === [[File:Oominakami shrine 20240713 0.jpg|thumb|Main shrine worship hall]] * First Torii Gate: Located beside the prefectural road. * Suisen Bridge * Second Torii Gate: Beyond this lies a dense sacred area. * Misogi purification site * Shimenawa Stone: The sacred rope at this shrine is also straight across the worship hall. * Zuishin Gate * Third Torii Gate * Shigure Lantern: Prefecturally designated cultural property * Main Shrine Worship Hall and Main Hall * Ema Hall, Archery Range, Shrine Office * Unagi Fuchi (Ryūō Fuchi): Remains of rainmaking rituals. It is said that black and white {{ill|lt=eels|Q1422437|WD=Q1422437|1=Unagi}} live here. If a black eel appears during rainmaking, rain will fall; if a white eel appears, there will be drought; and if a {{ill|lt=crab|Q40802|WD=Q40802|1=Brachyura}} appears, strong winds will come.From explanatory signboards installed throughout the shrine grounds, and below as well. * Iwakura (Meoto Iwa): Ancient sacred objects central to Oominakami worship. A stone shrine stands atop the lower rock of a two-tiered formation. * Oominakami Mountain Stream Valley: A dark, densely wooded valley along the Miyagawa River, a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Saita River|Q11634632|WD=Q11634632|1=Saita River}}. * Haiku and Tanka Monuments: {{ill|lt=Mizuhara Shūōshi|Q1940999|WD=Q1940999|1=Shūōshi Mizuhara}} “Though not famed as a tea region, none compare to its fresh tea”; {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Seishi|Q2599126|WD=Q2599126|1=Yamaguchi Seishi}} “Fine is the new tea, enveloped in the mist of Oominakami.” Inscription of Kōbō Daishi’s prayer: “May your coming and going be peaceful upon the sea of the sky; I, the pure waters above, am the Dragon Deity.” From the Ninomiya records. === Auxiliary and Subordinate Shrines === * Sen’go Ōji Shrine: Located up stone steps behind the main shrine grounds. A wooden shrine stands atop the lower rock beneath a three-tiered iwakura. Enshrines Izanagi no Mikoto and Izanami no Mikoto. ** Musubi Shrine: Enshrines the deity of binding (Musubi), connecting all prayers. * Shishamiya: Located beside the mikoshi storehouse on the left of the main grounds. Enshrines Taira no Norimori, Taira no Tsunemori, Taira no Sukemori, and Taira no Arimori. * Toyoha Shrine: Located on a hill to the left of the main grounds. Enshrines Aoba Ōkami, Kayano Hime no Mikoto, Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, and Sukunahikona no Mikoto. ** Ushigami Shrine: Enshrines Uke Mochi no Kami. ** Aramitama Shrine: Enshrines Ōmononushi no Kami. * Haraedo Shrine: Located to the right after passing through the Second Torii Gate. Enshrines Seoritsuhime no Kami, Hayakitsuhime no Kami, Ibukido Nushi no Kami, and Hayasasurahime no Kami. === Cultural Properties === ; National Historic Site * Ninomiya Kiln Ruins: Located at the mountain base outside the right side of the shrine grounds. Two kilns—No.1 (climbing kiln) on the right and No.2 (flat kiln) on the left—have yielded roof tiles, cups, and inkstones. A site spanning from the late Heian period to the Kamakura period. Designated April 25, 1932. ; Prefecturally Designated Tangible Cultural Property * Shigure Lantern: Total height 246 cm, made of tuff stone, created in 1346 (Kōei 4) by a craftsman of the highest rank “Hōkyō.” Designated April 9, 1963. ; Prefecturally Designated Natural Monument * Oominakami Shrine Juniper Tree: Located 20 meters north of the First Torii at the Otabisho. Height 12 m, branch spread 14 m, trunk circumference 4.4 m at breast height. Designated March 1976. === Access === From {{ill|lt=National Route 377|Q6157382|WD=Q6157382|1=Japan National Route 377}}, take {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefectural Road 218 Saita-Kamitakase Line|Q11667499|WD=Q11667499|1=Kagawa Prefectural Road Route 218}}. About 15 minutes from Sanuki-Toyonaka IC. Parking is available for 15 cars in front of the Zuishin Gate and 16 cars along the prefectural road in front of the First Torii. Entry to the shrine grounds is prohibited from 7:00 PM to around 5:00 AM due to the risk of wild boar sightings. === Notes === {{Notes help}} {{Reflist}} === External Links === {{Commonscat|Oominakami-jinja_(Mitoyo, Kagawa)}} * {{Instagram|minakaminomori}} * {{Facebook|ominakami}} * [Oominakami Shrine] - Mitoyo City {{Shinto navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Oominakami Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Oominakami Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Ni-no-Miya]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sanuki Province]] [[Category:香川県の神社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sanuki Province]] [[Category:香川県の旧県社]] [[Category:Municipal History of Mitoyo]] [[Category:Sanuki Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Mitoyo, Kagawa]] [[Category:pages]]
